Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

Shadworth Hodgson

1832 – 1912

A patient metaphysician of experience who helped name the philosophy of mind.

Shadworth Hollway Hodgson, 1832–1912, was an English metaphysician and epistemologist who worked largely outside university institutions but was highly respected in late Victorian philosophy; he was elected the first president of the Aristotelian Society in 1880 and held that office for thirteen successive years. His main systematic work, The Metaphysic of Experience, appeared in four volumes in 1898: Book I on the general analysis of experience, Book II on positive science, Book III on conscious action, and Book IV on the real universe. That division is important, because Hodgson’s “philosophy of mind” is not contained in one treatise under that title. It must be reconstructed from his metaphysics of experience, his theory of psychology as a positive science, his account of conscious action, and his guarded reflections on religion and the “unseen” world.

The best overall label for Hodgson’s view is: phenomenological realism with neuro-cerebral epiphenomenalism. It is phenomenological because philosophical method begins with the analysis of experience as it is given. It is realist because Hodgson does not reduce reality to consciousness; he argues that consciousness gives evidence of real conditions independent of consciousness. It is neuro-cerebral because the proximate real conditions of conscious life are, for human beings, the living nervous system and brain. It is epiphenomenalist because consciousness is conditioned by bodily and inter-bodily processes but does not itself condition them in the order of physical agency.

1. Method: begin with experience, not with soul, ego, brain, or external object

Hodgson’s first philosophical rule is anti-dogmatic: philosophy must begin with what is present in consciousness, and it must not smuggle in assumptions about subject, object, soul, matter, cause, or agency. He says philosophy is “subjective in its method” because it is an “interrogation of consciousness by consciousness,” consciousness being the only medium through which being is known to us. This does not mean subjective idealism. It means that evidence must first be found in the analysis of conscious content.

This methodological decision is the key to his entire philosophy of mind. Hodgson deliberately postpones questions such as: How is experience possible? What gives rise to it? What does it do? Who or what are “we,” the subjects of it? The terms self, ego, subject, agent, bearer of consciousness, cause, and condition are not allowed to function as explanatory primitives. They are initially only designative expressions drawn from common sense; they must acquire philosophical meaning through analysis.

This is why Hodgson sharply distinguishes metaphysics from psychology. Metaphysics first analyzes experience as “process-content.” Psychology comes later, when we ask genetic and causal questions about how conscious states arise, develop, and are conditioned. In Hodgson’s ordering, psychology is not false or secondary in value, but it is methodologically posterior: it presupposes a conception of the real subject and of real conditions that metaphysical analysis must first clarify.

The importance of this move is hard to overstate. Hodgson is rejecting both a Cartesian start from an ego-substance and a crude empiricist start from mental atoms. He is also rejecting the Hegelian move of treating thought or consciousness as an independent productive agency. The first datum is not “I think,” nor “brain produces sensation,” nor “external objects cause ideas,” but a content-process of experience that can be analyzed before one decides what subject, object, cause, or mind means.

2. Consciousness as “process-content”

Hodgson’s central descriptive unit is not a mental atom, an idea, a representation, or a faculty, but a process-content. Every actual bit of experience has both whatness and occurrence, content and process. The content is what is experienced; the process is the temporally occurring awareness of it. These are distinguishable but inseparable. That principle—distinguishability without separability—is one of Hodgson’s master distinctions and is later used for consciousness and object, knowing and known, subject and object, quality and duration, mind and body.

His analysis of a heard sound is paradigmatic. A single note presents sense-quality and duration. The sense-quality is the “material” element; duration is the “formal” element. Neither is found alone in actual experience. This lets Hodgson resist associationist atomism: the given is never a punctual, durationless sensation that later gets externally linked to others. The given already has internal temporality.

That leads to one of his most important contributions to philosophy of mind: the empirical present is not a knife-edge instant. Hodgson argues that the empirical present moment is the only immediate experience that actually exists for analysis, and that the supposed succession of such moments is an inference drawn from the structure of the present itself. The present has duration; it is not a mathematical point.

Retention is built into this durational present. Hodgson calls it “memory in its lowest terms,” not in the ordinary sense of later recollection, but as the retained presence without which duration could not be experienced. In a temporal experience, what has just occurred is retained within the present process. Thus memory is not merely a later act added to perception; minimal retention is constitutive of perception itself.

This gives Hodgson an early and sophisticated theory of temporal consciousness. Later discussions of the “specious present” often center on William James, but James drew on Hodgson and Robert Kelly, and recent scholarship describes Hodgson and Kelly as independent inventors of the specious-present theory. Hodgson, Kelly, and James all hold, in contrast to a punctual view, that our experiential present has duration.

3. Reflection, objectification, and the birth of subject-object structure

For Hodgson, reflective consciousness is not an optional higher-order act added to an already formed subject-object world. It is the process through which consciousness becomes object to itself. In his example of a sequence of notes, the moment occupied by note D includes both the simple experience of D and a retrospective experience of note C. The earlier moment is objectified in the later one; what was lived as process becomes known as object.

This matters for the philosophy of mind because Hodgson refuses to treat “subject” and “object” as primitive correlates. The subject-object distinction is a reflective achievement. Consciousness first occurs as process-content; then, in retrospection, one portion of experience becomes object for another. At that point one can distinguish content, awareness, existence, subjectivity, and objectivity. But these distinctions are not the starting point.

This gives Hodgson a view that resembles later phenomenology more than it resembles early analytic philosophy. The Encyclopedia of Philosophy notes that he resembles Husserl more than Russell or Moore, especially in his doctrine that things are what they are “known as” and in his method of distinguishing inseparables. The resemblance should not be overstated: Hodgson is not a Husserlian transcendental idealist. But his method is undeniably proto-phenomenological in treating consciousness as a field whose own structures must be described before one explains them causally.

4. Mind is not consciousness

Hodgson addressed the terminological issue directly in his 1887 Aristotelian Society paper, “Is Mind Synonymous with Consciousness?” His answer is no. He begins from a quasi-Cartesian schema: “some one’s consciousness of something,” or, in Cartesian terms, res cogitans, cogitatio, and cogitata. The question is whether “mind” belongs with the thinking subject or with consciousness itself. Hodgson argues that ordinary usage links mind with agency, whereas consciousness names a knowing or awareness, not an agent. To identify mind with consciousness would therefore favor, without argument, the hypothesis that consciousness itself has agency or is subject and consciousness in one.

His position is subtle. In one direction, mind is larger than consciousness, because many operations commonly called mental are partly or wholly below the threshold of consciousness, including motor and efferent nervous operations that form one connected system with conscious processes. In another direction, consciousness is larger than mind, because consciousness as content includes all objects known, not merely minds. So mind and consciousness overlap, but they are not coextensive.

The result is a threefold distinction. Consciousness is knowing, awareness, or process-content. Mind is a term for a subject or system characterized by conscious and unconscious functions. Ego is also not identical with consciousness: as subject it is presupposed by consciousness, while as known self it is an object within consciousness. Hodgson explicitly extends his reasons against identifying mind and consciousness to the ego as well.

This is one of the most modern parts of Hodgson’s view. He separates phenomenal consciousness from the broader system of mind, and he separates both from the metaphysical fiction of an immediately given ego-substance. A contemporary analogy would be the distinction between phenomenal consciousness, cognitive/neural architecture, and self-representation. Hodgson does not use those terms, but the structure is recognizably similar.

5. The unconscious and the rejection of faculty psychology

Hodgson’s unconscious is not Freud’s dynamic unconscious. It is closer to a subpersonal, physiological, and functional unconscious: processes of brain and nerve that do not rise into awareness but belong to the same system as conscious perception, memory, action, and volition. This is why he can say mind is larger than consciousness.

He also rejects faculty psychology. Terms such as memory, imagination, attention, reason, understanding, and will name groups of conscious processes or conscious actions classified by similarity in experience. They do not name separate inner powers or single organs. Hodgson warns that it is a fallacy to make entities out of abstractions, whether one locates them in a “mind” as separate powers or in the brain as separate seats for general faculties.

This is not anti-scientific. Quite the contrary: Hodgson thinks psychology becomes a positive science precisely when it stops appealing to occult faculties and identifies the real conditions of conscious processes. His anti-faculty stance is therefore paired with a physiological program: mental functions must be explained by real neuro-cerebral mechanisms, but those mechanisms must not be mythologized into crude one-faculty/one-organ correspondences.

6. Psychology as positive science: the “How comes?” question

Once metaphysical analysis has clarified what consciousness, subject, object, and real condition mean, psychology can ask its own question: How do conscious states arise, continue, associate, and develop? Hodgson says psychology deals with consciousness as an existent knowing, objectified and considered in abstraction from what it knows, in its genesis through nerve-substance, its proximate real condition.

Psychology, for Hodgson, becomes a positive science by facing the question of real conditioning. It must adopt a definite, intelligible, and verifiable hypothesis about the agent and agency involved in consciousness. In the absence of a defensible metaphysical or ontological hypothesis of a special entity with faculties, biology supplies the required basis: nerve substance, the nervous system, and the energies displayed by living organisms.

Hodgson is explicit that the neuro-cerebral system is not merely a convenient accompaniment. Scientific psychologists admit it to be a sine qua non condition of consciousness, and Hodgson holds it to be sufficient in the relevant sense for the phenomena of consciousness as we know them. This does not mean that neural conditions explain the qualitative nature of awareness; it means they are the sufficient real conditions for the occurrence, order, and development of particular conscious states.

He gives three central cases where psychological explanation requires real conditions: sense-perception, spontaneous redintegration or association of ideas, and volitional modification. The laws of similarity and contiguity describe relations between conscious states, but they do not explain why a particular recall actually occurs. For that, there must be a relatively permanent mechanism. Similarly, volitional modification requires some real agency beyond the mere analysis of consciousness as process-content.

7. The body, brain, and the real subject

Hodgson’s “real subject” is not an incorporeal ego. In human psychology, conscious phenomena depend proximately on the individual’s neuro-cerebral system as part of a living organism interacting with an external physical world. He explicitly says he prefers that hypothesis to the hypothesis of a purely psychical agent.

He distinguishes two concomitant processes in conscious life: the process of consciousness or experience, and the neuro-cerebral process on which it depends for its genesis and for the order in which its process-contents occur. When experience is considered simply as knowing, it has a retrospective structure; when it is considered as an existent dependent on neuro-cerebral process, it belongs to a forward-moving order of genesis.

This yields a highly unusual combination. Hodgson is not a reductive materialist, because he never reduces the whatness of consciousness to matter. But he is also not an interactionist dualist, because he does not treat consciousness or soul as a real efficient agency acting on matter. The agent in the positive science of psychology is the living nervous organism.

8. Realism without reduction: matter conditions existence, not whatness

Hodgson’s philosophy of mind depends on a crucial distinction between the order of knowledge and the order of existence. In the order of knowledge, consciousness is first: all evidence appears in consciousness. In the order of existence, however, particular conscious states are conditioned by material processes. This lets him reject both idealism and reductive materialism.

His sharpest statement is that matter conditions the existence, occurrence, or continuance of conscious states, but not their whatness, quality, or content. No real condition can explain the specific nature of awareness itself or the specific qualities of immediate perceptions. Matter, taken as known through perception, accounts for the coming into existence of perceptions, but not for their being the specific perceptions they are.

This is an early version of what later philosophy of mind calls the explanatory gap. Hodgson does not deny dependence on the brain. He denies that real conditioning in the order of genesis explains the qualitative nature of consciousness. The existence of a tactile perception may be conditioned by matter, but the felt quality of touch is not explained by the primary properties of matter; rather, those material properties are themselves known through perception.

Thus Hodgson is a realist because he affirms real conditions independent of consciousness, but a phenomenological realist because all access to those real conditions is through what consciousness discloses. He says we cannot transcend the conception of material reality in framing any positive conception of a real condition, yet that very material reality is known only through the inseparable relation between existence and consciousness.

9. Epiphenomenalism, but not eliminativism

In contemporary terms, epiphenomenalism is the view that mental events are caused by physical events in the brain but have no causal effects on physical events. The classic nineteenth-century context includes Huxley’s comparison of consciousness to a steam whistle that contributes nothing to the work of a locomotive, and James’s famous rejection of that picture.

Hodgson belongs squarely to that nineteenth-century epiphenomenalist context, but his version is more nuanced than a simple automaton theory. The Encyclopedia of Philosophy summarizes him as holding that consciousness gives knowledge of a reality independent of consciousness, that consciousness is an epiphenomenon, and that the proximate causes of psychical events lie in the neuro-cerebral system; possible immaterial causes are not absolutely ruled out, but experience reveals none.

The British Academy memoir makes the same point in Hodgson’s own terms: consciousness “can be called an epiphenomenon,” but one must not forget that it is through consciousness alone that matter is known as a real condition. This is why his epiphenomenalism is not eliminativist. Consciousness is causally dependent, but epistemically indispensable. It is not the physical agent, but it is the only medium in which agency, matter, body, subject, and world are disclosed.

This creates a tension. If consciousness never conditions physical action, then reasons, intentions, and deliberations do not literally cause bodily movements. Hodgson’s likely answer is that conscious reasons are the conscious aspect of neuro-cerebral processes that do cause action. Still, the tension remains: his system must explain how rational and ethical life can be genuinely meaningful while consciousness is causally inert in the physical order.

10. Action, volition, and free will

Book III of The Metaphysic of Experience is explicitly devoted to the “Analysis of Conscious Action,” and Book IV continues with “The Foundations of Ethic.”

Hodgson’s view of action follows from his general mind-body theory. Volitions are conscious process-contents, but the real agency that carries action forward lies in the organism, especially the neuro-cerebral mechanism. He does not deny that we choose; he denies that a separate immaterial self is needed to explain choosing. In a physiological sense, conscious choices are products of cerebral volitional processes. In a practical and ethical sense, they are still our choices, because they occur within the organized conscious life of the individual.

The Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes his free-will position as compatibilist: we are free in the sense that we determine our own actions, but what determines them in each case is a set of neuro-cerebral conditions that is not itself self-determined, accompanied by consciousness. Our awareness of freedom is, on this reading, awareness of the uncertainty of the outcome of volition.

This is not libertarian agent-causal freedom. Nor is it a hard determinism that simply dismisses agency. It is a practical compatibilism grounded in a neuro-cerebral theory of real agency and a phenomenological theory of deliberative consciousness. Consciousness discloses alternatives, motives, and preferabilities; the organism, as real condition, carries action into the physical world.

11. Soul, spirit, and incorporeal mind

Hodgson’s stance on soul or incorporeal mind is austere. He rejects the explanatory use of a non-material bearer of consciousness unless one can form an intelligible conception of a non-material yet really operative existent. He says that this has not been done, even by those most convinced of such an entity.

He also says that in the positive science of psychology, biology supplies the operative hypothesis. Nerve tissue is the only tissue to which the facts plainly warrant us in referring consciousness. Later, he states even more provocatively that it is the nervous system that is really described by the popular and figurative terms “soul,” “mind,” and “spirit.”

So Hodgson’s psychology is anti-Cartesian. It does not allow the soul to function as an explanatory substance behind perception, memory, thought, or will. The “soul” of common language is, for positive psychology, the living nervous system considered as the real condition of conscious life.

Yet Hodgson is not a dogmatic metaphysical materialist. His rejection is directed at the claim that experience gives us warrant for an operative immaterial mind as a psychological cause. The Encyclopedia summary is careful: there might be immaterial causes of psychical events, but experience reveals none.

His philosophy of religion is more permissive, but in a practical rather than theoretical way. The British Academy memoir reports that Hodgson could think of the “unseen world” as a region of existent consciousness, similar in essence to ours but infinite and eternal, and that he allowed the thought of God as Person a kind of practical certainty, while denying speculative knowledge of God as a person.

The result is a two-level view. In psychology and philosophy of mind, Hodgson rejects incorporeal mind as an explanatory agent. In religious reflection, he permits a disciplined, practical conception of unseen consciousness or divine personality, but not as a piece of positive psychological science. This is a decisive break from traditional soul psychology.

12. Historical position in the development of philosophy of mind

Hodgson occupies a transitional position between several major currents. He stands between British associationism and British absolute idealism. Against the associationists, he rejects mental atomism and the idea that laws of association explain mental life. Against Hegelian idealism, he rejects the hypostatization of thought as a real productive agency. The Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes him as occupying a central position, attacking both associationist superficiality and Germanizing idealist vagueness.

He also belongs to the late nineteenth-century emergence of scientific psychology. He takes the nervous system seriously as the basis for psychological explanation, discusses psychophysical law, and treats psychology as a positive science grounded in biology. But he refuses to let psychology replace philosophy, because psychology already presupposes concepts—subject, object, condition, causation—that must be clarified by metaphysical analysis.

In philosophy of temporal consciousness, he is historically significant as a precursor to James. James recognized similarities and Hodgson’s priority, even where they differed metaphysically. Recent work on the specious present places Hodgson among the central late nineteenth-century figures who argued that the experiential present has duration.

In the mind-body debate, Hodgson should be placed beside Huxley and James. Like Huxley, he accepts a broadly epiphenomenalist view of consciousness’s causal role. Unlike a crude automaton theorist, however, he insists that consciousness is epistemically primary and qualitatively irreducible. Unlike James, he does not give consciousness a direct efficacious role in action.

In relation to later phenomenology, Hodgson anticipates certain themes: bracketing assumptions about subject and object, analyzing experience as given, distinguishing inseparable aspects, treating the present as durational, and taking selfhood as something disclosed in reflective consciousness rather than as a primitive substance. But he remains a realist and a physiological psychologist in a way that sharply separates him from Husserl’s transcendental project.

13. The extracted Hodgsonian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Hodgson can be stated as follows.

First, all philosophical evidence begins in consciousness, but this is methodological, not idealist. Consciousness is the medium of knowledge; it is not automatically the whole of being.

Second, the basic datum is process-content. Experience is not a set of discrete mental atoms but a temporally structured process in which content and awareness, quality and duration, are distinguishable but inseparable.

Third, the empirical present has duration. Immediate consciousness is not a punctual instant; it contains retention, or memory in its lowest terms. Temporal consciousness is therefore internally structured.

Fourth, subject-object structure is derivative. Consciousness becomes object to itself through retrospection; the ego and subject are not initial data but later reflective articulations.

Fifth, mind and consciousness are not synonymous. Consciousness is knowing; mind names a subject or system that includes unconscious operations. Mind is larger than consciousness in one respect; consciousness is larger than mind in another, because consciousness includes all known objects.

Sixth, the self is not a Cartesian soul. The self or ego is partly an object within consciousness and partly the presupposed subject of conscious functioning; it cannot be identified with consciousness itself, nor can it be posited as an unexplained immaterial cause.

Seventh, psychology is the science of the genesis and history of consciousness as an existent. It asks how conscious states arise and are ordered, and it must adopt real, verifiable conditions rather than occult faculties.

Eighth, the proximate real condition of human consciousness is the neuro-cerebral organism. The nervous system, as part of a living body in interaction with the physical world, is the real subject for positive psychology.

Ninth, consciousness is epiphenomenal in the physical order. It is conditioned by organic and interorganic processes and does not condition them. But it is not unreal or eliminable, because it is the only medium through which the body, world, and their relations are known.

Tenth, material conditions explain occurrence, not qualitative whatness. Matter accounts for the coming-to-existence of perceptions but not for their specific felt or conscious character. This makes Hodgson an early theorist of the explanatory gap.

Eleventh, agency is practical and organismic, not soul-causal. We deliberate, choose, and act, but the real determining conditions are neuro-cerebral and bodily. Free will is compatible with determinism because freedom is not uncaused agency; it is the practical structure of action within an organism whose determining conditions are not themselves self-determined.

Twelfth, religious ideas of soul, unseen consciousness, or divine personality are not psychological explanations. Hodgson permits them, if at all, as practical or religious interpretations, not as positive theoretical causes of mental life.

14. Critical assessment

Hodgson’s greatest strength is methodological discipline. He sees that philosophy of mind cannot begin by assuming the very entities it needs to explain: ego, subject, soul, external object, brain, causation, and agency. His insistence on first analyzing process-content gives him a powerful alternative to both Cartesianism and associationism.

His second major strength is the analysis of time-consciousness. The empirical present, retention, and reflective objectification give him a theory that anticipates later debates over the specious present and internal time-consciousness. This alone makes him historically important.

His third strength is his combination of realism and irreducibility. He accepts neuro-cerebral dependence without reducing qualitative consciousness to matter. The distinction between the existence of consciousness and its whatness is one of the most philosophically durable parts of his system.

The main weakness is the familiar problem of epiphenomenalism. If consciousness has no causal role in bodily action, then it becomes difficult to explain how reasoning, deliberation, moral insight, and philosophical analysis make any practical difference. Hodgson can say that the real cause is neuro-cerebral and that conscious reasoning is the known aspect of that process; but this leaves a deep question about the status of rational agency.

A second weakness concerns the relation between phenomenological method and theological extension. Hodgson is rigorous when refusing incorporeal mind as a psychological explanation. His later allowance for an unseen world or divine personality as practical certainty is more difficult to integrate with the evidential austerity of his method.

Even with these tensions, Hodgson deserves a more central place in the history of philosophy of mind. He is a late nineteenth-century figure who moves decisively beyond soul psychology, anticipates phenomenological description, develops a sophisticated theory of temporal consciousness, accepts neurophysiological dependence, and formulates an early version of the explanatory gap. His philosophy of mind is not Cartesian dualism, not reductive physicalism, not absolute idealism, and not mere empiricism. It is a distinctive and historically significant attempt to do justice to consciousness as both epistemically primordial and physically conditioned.
